events.text: The patient is a 58-year-old male named Gerald T. Holloway. He is a former smoker (quit 12 years prior, 20 pack-year history), works as a civil engineer, and has a history of hypertension (well-controlled on lisinopril 10 mg daily prior to diagnosis) and hyperlipidemia (on atorvastatin). He has no personal or family history of colorectal cancer and was not up to date on colorectal cancer screening.

At age 58 years, the patient had a diagnosis of Stage IVA (AJCC 8th edition) ADENOCARCINOMA, NOS of the SIGMOID COLON (ICD-O-3 site code C187, histology code 8140/3). TNM staging: pT3 pN2a M1a. Summary stage: Distant (metastatic). The primary tumor was a 4.8 cm moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma of the sigmoid colon. Synchronous liver metastases were present (4 lesions, largest 3.1 cm in segment VI). No peritoneal carcinomatosis. Confirmation: POSITIVE HISTOLOGY via colonoscopic biopsy. MSI status: MSS (microsatellite stable) by IHC (MLH1+, MSH2+, MSH6+, PMS2+). KRAS mutation: G12D (codon 12). NRAS: wild-type. BRAF: wild-type (V600E negative). HER2: not amplified. CEA at diagnosis: 187 ng/mL. CA 19-9 at diagnosis: 94 U/mL.

At age 58 years, the patient had a colonoscopic biopsy of the sigmoid colon mass. Pathology result: Moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma, consistent with colorectal primary. Tumor shows conventional glandular architecture with focal dirty necrosis. IHC for mismatch repair proteins: MLH1 retained, MSH2 retained, MSH6 retained, PMS2 retained -- consistent with microsatellite-stable (MSS) tumor. KRAS sequencing (hotspot panel): KRAS p.G12D (c.35G>A) mutation detected. NRAS exons 2-4: wild-type. BRAF V600E: wild-type.

At age 58 years, the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast (staging CT). Findings: Primary sigmoid colon mass measuring 4.8 x 3.9 cm causing partial luminal narrowing without obstruction. Liver: 4 hypodense metastatic lesions identified -- segment VI (3.1 cm), segment IVb (2.2 cm), segment II (1.4 cm), segment VII (0.9 cm). No extrahepatic abdominal disease. No peritoneal implants. Chest: No pulmonary nodules or metastases identified. No mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Impression: Stage IVA colorectal cancer with synchronous hepatic metastases. No pulmonary metastases at this time.

At age 58 years, the patient had next generation sequencing (comprehensive solid tumor panel, 500+ gene panel) performed on the sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma biopsy specimen. Tumor mutational burden (TMB): 6 mut/Mb (low). MSI status by NGS: MSS. Actionable alterations: KRAS p.G12D (variant allele frequency 41%, known oncogenic driver). Comutations and additional findings: APC p.R1450* (frameshift, loss of function, variant allele frequency 44%, founder mutation consistent with sporadic CRC), TP53 p.R175H (gain-of-function hotspot, VAF 39%), SMAD4 wild-type, PIK3CA wild-type, PTEN wild-type. Copy number alterations: MYC gain (8q24 amplification, copy number 6), CDK8 gain (13q12), chromosome 20q gain. No actionable fusions detected (ALK, RET, NTRK1/2/3, ROS1 all negative). No HER2 amplification (copy number 2.1). BRCA1/2: wild-type. ATM: wild-type. POLE/POLD1: wild-type (consistent with MSS, low TMB). RAS pathway: KRAS G12D oncogenic; NRAS wild-type; HRAS wild-type; NF1 wild-type. Summary: KRAS-mutant (G12D), MSS, BRAF wild-type colorectal adenocarcinoma. Not eligible for anti-EGFR therapy. KRAS G12D inhibitors under investigation in clinical trials.

At age 58 years, the patient underwent laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy with primary anastomosis (low anterior resection approach) for the primary sigmoid colon tumor. Operative findings: Sigmoid mass as expected, no peritoneal disease encountered, liver metastases grossly consistent with pre-operative imaging. Procedure uncomplicated. Final pathology: pT3 pN2a (3/18 lymph nodes positive for metastatic adenocarcinoma), R0 resection (proximal and distal margins negative, radial margin 4 mm). Lymphovascular invasion: present. Perineural invasion: present. Tumor deposit: 1 identified.

At age 58 years, the patient had a surgical pathology report from the sigmoid colectomy specimen. Diagnosis: Moderately differentiated invasive adenocarcinoma of sigmoid colon, pT3 pN2a. Tumor size: 4.8 x 3.9 x 3.2 cm. Histologic grade: G2 (moderately differentiated). Depth of invasion: through muscularis propria into pericolorectal tissues (pT3), no penetration of visceral peritoneum. Lymph nodes: 3 of 18 positive for metastatic adenocarcinoma (pN2a). Largest nodal metastasis: 1.1 cm. Extranodal extension: absent. Margins: proximal (negative, 8 cm), distal (negative, 6 cm), radial (negative, 4 mm clearance). Lymphovascular invasion: present (extensive). Perineural invasion: present. Tumor deposits: 1. Tumor budding: high (Bd3). Background: no adenomatous polyps in remaining specimen.

At age 58 years, the patient had a medical oncology consultation approximately 5 weeks post-operatively. He had recovered well from sigmoid colectomy. ECOG performance status: 1. Discussion of systemic therapy options for metastatic colorectal cancer with KRAS G12D mutation. Given MSS status and KRAS mutation, patient is not eligible for anti-EGFR therapy (cetuximab/panitumumab) or immunotherapy. Resectability of liver metastases reviewed with hepatobiliary surgery: potentially resectable after cytoreductive systemic therapy. Plan for FOLFOX plus bevacizumab as first-line systemic therapy, with intent to reassess liver metastases for potential resection after 6-8 cycles. Patient counseled regarding expected toxicities including neuropathy, nausea, myelosuppression, and bevacizumab-associated risks (hypertension, wound healing). Cancer present. Beginning first-line treatment.

At age 58 years, the patient initiated first-line systemic therapy with FOLFOX6 (modified) plus bevacizumab for metastatic colorectal cancer (KRAS-mutant, MSS, RAS wild-type for anti-EGFR ineligibility). Regimen: oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2 IV day 1, leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV day 1, 5-fluorouracil 400 mg/m2 IV bolus day 1, 5-fluorouracil 2400 mg/m2 IV continuous infusion over 46 hours, bevacizumab 5 mg/kg IV day 1; every 14 days. Treatment intent: palliative/disease control with potential conversion to resectability. Baseline CEA: 187 ng/mL. Baseline CA 19-9: 94 U/mL.

At age 58 years, after cycle 2 of FOLFOX/bevacizumab, the patient reported grade 1 peripheral sensory neuropathy (mild tingling in fingertips, no functional impairment), grade 1-2 nausea (managed with ondansetron and prochlorperazine), and grade 1 fatigue. No dose modifications required. Blood pressure was noted to be elevated at clinic visit (148/92 mmHg) compared to prior baseline; home blood pressure monitoring initiated. CTCAE grading: neuropathy grade 1, nausea grade 2, hypertension grade 1. Lisinopril dose increased from 10 mg to 20 mg daily.

At age 58 years (approximately 3 months after starting FOLFOX/bevacizumab, following cycle 6), the patient had a restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast. Findings: Primary sigmoid colon resection site unremarkable, no local recurrence. Liver metastases: partial response by RECIST 1.1 criteria -- segment VI lesion reduced from 3.1 cm to 1.9 cm (-39%), segment IVb from 2.2 cm to 1.4 cm (-36%), segment II from 1.4 cm to 0.8 cm (-43%), segment VII from 0.9 cm to 0.5 cm (-44%). Sum of target lesion diameters: reduced from 7.6 cm to 4.6 cm (overall -39.5%, partial response). No new lesions. Chest: still no pulmonary nodules. CEA trending down. Impression: Partial response to FOLFOX/bevacizumab.

At age 58 years, after cycle 7-8 of FOLFOX/bevacizumab, the patient developed worsening peripheral sensory neuropathy now at grade 2 (moderate -- persistent paresthesias in hands and feet, interfering with fine motor tasks such as buttoning shirts, mild difficulty walking on uneven surfaces). Hypertension worsened (blood pressure readings consistently 155-165/95-100 mmHg despite lisinopril 20 mg). Amlodipine 5 mg daily added to antihypertensive regimen. Oxaliplatin dose reduced by 25% (from 85 mg/m2 to 65 mg/m2) per protocol for grade 2 neuropathy. No changes to 5-FU or bevacizumab dosing. CEA at this time: 52 ng/mL (continued decline).

At age 58 years (after cycle 8 of FOLFOX/bevacizumab), the patient had a hepatobiliary surgery multidisciplinary tumor board review. Imaging reviewed: partial response in all 4 hepatic metastases, but lesions not yet reduced to resectable size with adequate future liver remnant. Recommend continuation of systemic therapy for additional 4 cycles (to total 12 cycles) with repeat hepatic resectability assessment. ECOG PS: 1. Cancer present, responding to therapy. Neuropathy grade 2 with dose-reduced oxaliplatin. Hypertension grade 2 now better controlled on dual antihypertensive therapy.

At age 58 years, after cycle 10 of FOLFOX/bevacizumab, neuropathy progressed to grade 2-3 (severe tingling and numbness in bilateral hands and feet, significant functional impairment -- unable to reliably grasp objects, unsteady gait on uneven ground, difficulty with daily activities). Decision made by the treating oncologist to discontinue oxaliplatin after cycle 10 (rather than completing 12 cycles with oxaliplatin) and continue bevacizumab and 5-FU as maintenance therapy. Hypertension: blood pressure now 138/85 mmHg on lisinopril 20 mg plus amlodipine 5 mg -- acceptably controlled.

At age 58 years (approximately 5.5 months after starting therapy, after cycle 10 of FOLFOX/bevacizumab), the patient had a restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis. Findings: Continued partial response -- segment VI liver lesion 1.5 cm (from 3.1 cm baseline, -52%), segment IVb 1.0 cm (-55%), segment II 0.5 cm (-64%), segment VII 0.4 cm (-56%). Sum of target lesion diameters: 3.4 cm (baseline 7.6 cm, -55% overall). Hepatobiliary surgery re-reviewed: technically resectable, but oncology team and patient elected to continue systemic therapy to consolidate response further. No new lesions. Chest: no pulmonary metastases. CEA: 28 ng/mL. CA 19-9: 18 U/mL. Impression: Ongoing partial response to treatment. Transitioned to maintenance 5-FU/bevacizumab given oxaliplatin-related neuropathy.

At age 58 years (after cycle 10 of FOLFOX/bevacizumab, approximately 5.5 months from treatment start), the patient transitioned to maintenance therapy with 5-fluorouracil plus bevacizumab due to cumulative oxaliplatin-related neuropathy. Regimen: leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV day 1, 5-fluorouracil 400 mg/m2 IV bolus day 1, 5-fluorouracil 2400 mg/m2 IV continuous infusion 46 hours, bevacizumab 5 mg/kg IV day 1; every 14 days. Continuation of disease control intent. Neuropathy expected to gradually improve off oxaliplatin.

At age 58 years (approximately 7 months from diagnosis), the patient had an oncology follow-up visit during maintenance 5-FU/bevacizumab. He reported gradual but incomplete improvement in peripheral neuropathy (still grade 1-2, now primarily in feet). Tolerated maintenance regimen well -- grade 1 fatigue, no significant nausea, blood pressure stable on dual antihypertensives. CEA: 22 ng/mL (stable). ECOG PS: 1. Cancer present but stable on maintenance therapy. Discussed ongoing liver metastasis resectability; patient and team elected to continue systemic therapy given incomplete response and grade 2 neuropathy recovery.

At age 58 years (approximately 10 months from initial treatment start, during maintenance 5-FU/bevacizumab), the patient had a restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis. Findings: Liver lesions -- stable/slight progression compared to prior: segment VI lesion now 1.8 cm (prior 1.5 cm, +20%), segment IVb 1.3 cm (prior 1.0 cm, +30%), segment II 0.7 cm (prior 0.5 cm), segment VII 0.5 cm (stable). New findings: 2 new pulmonary nodules identified -- right lower lobe 0.8 cm and right upper lobe 0.5 cm, both new compared to prior scans. Lymphadenopathy: no significant mediastinal/hilar lymphadenopathy. CEA: 41 ng/mL (rising from 22). CA 19-9: 34 U/mL (rising). Impression: Radiographic progression of disease -- hepatic lesion growth and new pulmonary metastases. Consistent with progressive disease (PD) by RECIST 1.1.

At age 59 years (10 months from start of treatment), the patient had an urgent oncology visit following radiographic progression. New pulmonary nodules confirmed by radiology as consistent with metastatic disease given clinical context (new bilateral lesions, rise in CEA). ECOG PS: 1. Cancer present, progressing. Discussion of second-line options: given KRAS G12D mutation, patient remains ineligible for anti-EGFR therapy. BRAF wild-type excludes BRAF-targeted combinations. MSS status excludes immune checkpoint inhibitor monotherapy. Options reviewed: FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab or bevacizumab continuation, FOLFIRI plus cetuximab (not applicable -- KRAS mutant), TAS-102 (trifluridine/tipiracil), regorafenib, or clinical trial (KRAS G12D-directed therapy if available). Decision: FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab as second-line therapy given good performance status, prior bevacizumab exposure (switching VEGF pathway targeting to VEGFR-2 with ramucirumab), and available evidence.

At age 59 years (approximately 10.5 months from initial treatment start), the patient initiated second-line systemic therapy with FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab for progressive metastatic colorectal cancer (KRAS G12D mutant, MSS). Regimen: ramucirumab 8 mg/kg IV day 1, irinotecan 180 mg/m2 IV day 1, leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV day 1, 5-fluorouracil 400 mg/m2 IV bolus day 1, 5-fluorouracil 2400 mg/m2 IV continuous infusion over 46 hours; every 14 days. Premedication: dexamethasone, diphenhydramine, ranitidine before irinotecan. Prior neuropathy from oxaliplatin (grade 1-2 residual) expected to remain separate from irinotecan toxicity profile. Baseline CEA: 41 ng/mL.

At age 59 years, after cycles 1-2 of FOLFIRI/ramucirumab, the patient developed grade 2 diarrhea (4-6 loose stools per day, manageable with loperamide), grade 2 alopecia (diffuse hair thinning -- expected with irinotecan), grade 2 fatigue, and grade 1 mucositis. Hypertension continued at baseline-controlled levels (blood pressure 135-140/88 mmHg on lisinopril 20 mg and amlodipine 5 mg). No grade 3-4 toxicity. No dose modifications required. UGT1A1 genotype was checked retrospectively: UGT1A1 *1/*28 (heterozygous), suggesting intermediate irinotecan metabolizer -- monitoring for delayed-onset diarrhea.

At age 59 years (approximately 3 months after starting FOLFIRI/ramucirumab, cycle 6 restaging), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast. Findings: Hepatic metastases -- partial response compared to prior progression imaging: segment VI 1.4 cm (from 1.8 cm, -22%), segment IVb 1.0 cm (from 1.3 cm, -23%), segment II 0.6 cm (stable), segment VII 0.4 cm (stable). Pulmonary nodules: right lower lobe 0.7 cm (from 0.8 cm, slight decrease), right upper lobe 0.5 cm (stable). No new lesions. Sum of target lesion diameters decreased by 17%. CEA: 26 ng/mL (declining from 41). CA 19-9: 22 U/mL. Impression: Partial response to second-line FOLFIRI/ramucirumab. Cancer present, responding.

At age 59 years (13 months from initial diagnosis), the patient had a scheduled oncology follow-up. He was tolerating FOLFIRI/ramucirumab reasonably well. Residual peripheral neuropathy from prior oxaliplatin had improved to grade 1 (mild persistent numbness in feet, not functionally limiting). Diarrhea managed with loperamide as needed. ECOG PS: 1. Blood pressure: 138/86 mmHg (adequately controlled). CEA trending down (26 ng/mL). Discussed goals of care: patient remains motivated for disease control; hepatic resectability reassessed by hepatobiliary surgery -- currently not favored given pulmonary disease (bilateral extent of metastatic disease). Plan to continue FOLFIRI/ramucirumab with next restaging at 3-month intervals. Cancer present, responding to second-line therapy.

At age 59 years (cycle 8-9 of FOLFIRI/ramucirumab), the patient developed grade 3 hypertension (blood pressure consistently 168-178/102-110 mmHg at home and clinic, despite dual antihypertensive therapy). This was attributed primarily to ongoing anti-angiogenic therapy (ramucirumab). Ramucirumab dose held for one cycle; amlodipine dose increased to 10 mg daily, and hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg added. Blood pressure improved to 148/90 mmHg over 2 weeks. Ramucirumab resumed at full dose with blood pressure monitoring at every visit. CEA at this time: 24 ng/mL (stable). CTCAE grade 3 hypertension, managed with medication adjustment and brief hold.

At age 59 years (approximately 6 months after starting FOLFIRI/ramucirumab, cycle 12 restaging, 16 months from initial diagnosis), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast. Findings: Hepatic metastases stable compared to prior: segment VI 1.3 cm, segment IVb 1.0 cm, segment II 0.5 cm, segment VII 0.3 cm. Pulmonary nodules: right lower lobe 0.7 cm (stable), right upper lobe 0.4 cm (slight decrease). No new lesions in liver, lungs, peritoneum, or bone. CEA: 22 ng/mL. CA 19-9: 19 U/mL. Impression: Stable disease to ongoing partial response -- disease control maintained on FOLFIRI/ramucirumab. Cancer present but controlled.

At age 59 years (approximately 16 months from initial diagnosis), the patient had an oncology office visit. He had been on FOLFIRI/ramucirumab for 6 months (12 cycles). Disease remains controlled per most recent CT (stable disease). ECOG PS: 1. Neuropathy: grade 1 (residual numbness in feet, stable and not worsening). Hypertension: grade 1-2, controlled on triple antihypertensive therapy (lisinopril 20 mg, amlodipine 10 mg, hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg). Labs: CBC within acceptable parameters (absolute neutrophil count 2,100/uL, hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL -- mild anemia of chronic disease), BMP within normal limits, LFTs mildly elevated (ALT 52 U/L, AST 48 U/L). Patient fatigued from cumulative therapy. Discussed treatment holidays and continued management. Plan: continue current regimen, reassess with next CT in 3 months. Cancer present, stable.

At age 59 years (approximately 19 months from initial diagnosis, cycle 18 of FOLFIRI/ramucirumab), the patient had a restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis. Findings: Hepatic metastases: segment VI 1.5 cm (slight increase from 1.3 cm), segment IVb 1.2 cm (slight increase from 1.0 cm), segment II 0.6 cm (slight increase from 0.5 cm), segment VII 0.4 cm (slight increase). Sum of target lesion diameters: 3.7 cm (versus nadir 3.0 cm on this regimen, +23%). Pulmonary nodules: right lower lobe 0.9 cm (new growth from 0.7 cm), right upper lobe 0.5 cm. No new sites of metastatic disease. CEA: 58 ng/mL (rising from 22). CA 19-9: 47 U/mL (rising). Impression: Progressive disease by RECIST 1.1 -- hepatic and pulmonary lesion growth with rising tumor markers. Cancer present, progressing on second-line therapy.

At age 59 years (19 months from initial diagnosis), the patient had an urgent oncology visit following CT showing progression on FOLFIRI/ramucirumab. ECOG PS: 1-2 (some decline in functional status, increased fatigue). Cancer present, progressing. Review of all prior therapies: first-line FOLFOX/bevacizumab (partial response, then neuropathy-limited), maintenance 5-FU/bevacizumab (progression at 10 months), second-line FOLFIRI/ramucirumab (partial response then progression at 9 months). Options for further therapy: TAS-102 (trifluridine/tipiracil), regorafenib, clinical trial enrollment for KRAS G12D-directed therapy (patient referred to tertiary center for trial eligibility screening). Patient and family counseled regarding disease trajectory; goals of care discussion initiated -- patient wishes to continue active treatment if reasonable options available. Referral placed to clinical trial unit at regional cancer center for KRAS G12D inhibitor trial screening.

At age 59 years (approximately 19.5 months from initial diagnosis), the patient enrolled in a Phase I/II clinical trial evaluating a KRAS G12D-selective inhibitor (investigational agent) for patients with KRAS G12D-mutant solid tumors including colorectal cancer. Patient screened eligible: KRAS G12D confirmed by prior NGS, ECOG PS 1-2, adequate organ function. Trial regimen per protocol: oral investigational KRAS G12D inhibitor at assigned dose level plus standard supportive care. Prior lines of therapy: 2 (FOLFOX/bev, FOLFIRI/ramucirumab). Trial site: regional cancer center. Baseline tumor biopsy obtained for correlative studies (right lower lobe pulmonary nodule, CT-guided biopsy).

At age 59 years, the patient had a CT-guided biopsy of the right lower lobe pulmonary nodule for clinical trial enrollment (re-biopsy at progression). Pathology result: Metastatic adenocarcinoma consistent with colorectal primary (morphology and IHC: CK20+, CDX2+, CK7 focally+, TTF-1 negative). Molecular testing: KRAS G12D mutation confirmed (VAF 38%). KRAS G12C: not detected. No new acquired resistance mutations identified (NRAS, BRAF V600E, KRAS amplification all negative). MSI by IHC: MSS (all 4 MMR proteins retained). This confirms persistent KRAS G12D-driven disease without secondary resistance mechanisms at the time of trial enrollment.

At age 59 years (approximately 21 months from initial diagnosis, cycle 3 of investigational KRAS G12D inhibitor trial), the patient had a protocol-specified tumor assessment visit. He tolerated the investigational oral agent well -- grade 1 nausea, grade 1 rash (acneiform, managed with topical clindamycin), grade 1 fatigue. ECOG PS: 1. Blood pressure: 135/84 mmHg (continued on triple antihypertensives). Residual neuropathy: grade 1 (improved, primarily in feet). CEA: 42 ng/mL (declined from 58 at trial entry). CA 19-9: 35 U/mL. Preliminary response assessment pending next CT. Cancer present; early marker response noted on investigational therapy. Patient expressing cautious optimism.

documents.text:
CT CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
RESTAGING EXAMINATION

CLINICAL INDICATION: 58-year-old male with known Stage IVA sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma, KRAS G12D-mutant, microsatellite stable. History of laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy with R0 resection (pT3pN2a). Four synchronous hepatic metastases identified on baseline staging CT. Currently on first-line systemic therapy with mFOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab; this examination performed following completion of cycle 6 for restaging and response assessment. CEA trending downward per clinical notes.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis acquired following the administration of intravenous nonionic iodinated contrast (100 mL Omnipaque 350 injected at 3 mL/sec) in portal venous phase. Oral contrast not administered per department protocol for oncologic restaging. Axial images obtained at 1.25 mm slice thickness with coronal and sagittal multiplanar reformats reviewed. No prior allergic reactions to contrast on file.

COMPARISON: Staging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis performed approximately 3 months prior (prior to initiation of systemic therapy).

--- FINDINGS ---

CHEST:

Lungs and airways: The lungs are well inflated. No pulmonary nodules, consolidation, ground-glass opacities, or pleural-based lesions are identified. The airways are patent to the subsegmental level bilaterally. Importantly, no new pulmonary nodules are present compared to baseline examination. No endobronchial lesions.

Pleura: No pleural effusions bilaterally. No pneumothorax. No pleural thickening.

Mediastinum and hila: The mediastinum is midline. No pathologically enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes. The great vessels are normal in caliber. The heart is normal in size without pericardial effusion. The thoracic aorta is unremarkable.

Chest wall and osseous structures: No suspicious lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions identified in the visualized thoracic skeleton. Mild multilevel degenerative changes of the thoracic spine are noted, age appropriate.

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is normal in size and contour. Four previously identified hepatic metastatic lesions are again noted and demonstrate interval size reduction compared to the baseline staging examination:

-- Segment VI lesion: Previously measuring 3.1 cm in greatest axial dimension, now measures approximately 1.9 cm. This lesion retains its hypodense appearance on portal venous phase and remains well-circumscribed. There is no evidence of central necrosis or cavitation. Substantial reduction in size compared to baseline.

-- Segment IVb lesion: Previously 2.2 cm, now measuring approximately 1.4 cm. The lesion is hypodense and well-defined on current imaging. Notable interval decrease in size.

-- Segment II lesion: Previously 1.4 cm, now measuring approximately 0.8 cm. This lesion is at the threshold of reliable characterization but remains identifiable as a discrete hypodense focus and is clearly smaller than on prior examination.

-- Segment VII lesion: The smallest lesion, previously 0.9 cm, now measures approximately 0.5 cm. While small and near the limit of confident measurement, it appears reduced in size compared to baseline.

No new hepatic lesions are identified. There is no capsular retraction or ductal dilatation to suggest underlying fibrosis or biliary involvement. The hepatic veins and portal venous system are patent without evidence of tumor thrombosis. Background liver parenchyma is homogeneous without evidence of fatty infiltration, cirrhosis, or parenchymal injury that would suggest hepatotoxicity from systemic therapy.

Biliary system: The gallbladder is present and unremarkable without stones, wall thickening, or pericholecystic fluid. The common bile duct is not dilated, measuring 4 mm.

Spleen: Normal size and attenuation. No splenic lesions identified.

Pancreas: The pancreas demonstrates normal morphology and enhancement pattern throughout its entirety. The pancreatic duct is not dilated. No peripancreatic stranding or fluid collections.

Adrenal glands: The bilateral adrenal glands are normal in size and morphology. No adrenal masses or nodules to suggest metastatic involvement.

Kidneys and ureters: Both kidneys are normal in size with homogeneous enhancement and no filling defects. No hydronephrosis. No renal calculi or masses. The ureters are not dilated.

Vasculature: The abdominal aorta is normal in caliber throughout. The celiac axis, superior and inferior mesenteric arteries are patent. No lymphadenopathy in the celiac, portocaval, para-aortic, or mesenteric nodal stations. No grossly enlarged lymph nodes above the 1 cm short-axis threshold.

Gastrointestinal tract: Post-surgical changes consistent with sigmoid colectomy with primary anastomosis are present in the expected location. The anastomosis appears intact without surrounding fluid, soft tissue thickening, or air suggesting leak or abscess. There is no evidence of obstruction or ileus. No recurrent soft tissue mass or nodularity at the resection site. No pericolonic stranding or lymphadenopathy to suggest local recurrence. The remaining visualized large bowel and small intestine are unremarkable.

PERITONEUM AND PELVIS:

Peritoneum: No ascites. No peritoneal implants, omental caking, or soft tissue thickening along peritoneal surfaces to suggest peritoneal carcinomatosis. The mesentery is without nodularity.

Pelvis: Pelvic organs are unremarkable. No pelvic lymphadenopathy. No pelvic fluid collections. The urinary bladder is partially distended and unremarkable. No pelvic bone lesions.

MUSCULOSKELETAL:

No suspicious lytic or sclerotic lesions identified in the visualized axial and appendicular skeleton. Mild degenerative changes at the lumbar facet joints and disc spaces are noted, consistent with patient age. No compression fractures.

SOFT TISSUES:

No soft tissue masses or subcutaneous nodules identified. Postsurgical changes from prior abdominal surgery are noted in the left lower quadrant and hypogastric region.

--- IMPRESSION ---

1. Meaningful interval reduction in size of all four known hepatic metastatic lesions compared to the baseline staging examination obtained prior to the initiation of first-line systemic therapy. The largest lesion in segment VI has reduced from 3.1 cm to approximately 1.9 cm; the segment IVb lesion from 2.2 cm to 1.4 cm; the segment II lesion from 1.4 cm to 0.8 cm; and the segment VII lesion from 0.9 cm to 0.5 cm. Collectively, these findings are consistent with a meaningful therapeutic response in the hepatic disease burden. All lesions remain hypodense without evidence of complete radiographic resolution.

2. No new hepatic lesions. No new sites of intra-abdominal or pelvic metastatic disease.

3. Resection site and anastomosis appear intact without evidence of local recurrence or anastomotic complication.

4. The lungs remain clear. No pulmonary nodules identified. No thoracic lymphadenopathy.

5. No peritoneal implants or ascites. No osseous metastases identified in the imaged skeleton.

Overall, the imaging findings are consistent with a favorable response to ongoing systemic therapy. Continued follow-up with serial cross-sectional imaging recommended per treating oncologist's judgment. Hepatic resectability should be assessed by a multidisciplinary team in the context of evolving lesion size, hepatic reserve, and treatment trajectory.

Dictated by: Attending Radiologist, Abdominal Imaging Division
Electronically verified and signed.

MEDICAL ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Multidisciplinary Hepatobiliary Tumor Board Review / Treatment Assessment

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: [Medical Oncology Attending]
SERVICE: Medical Oncology / GI Oncology
VISIT TYPE: Outpatient Follow-Up, Post-Cycle 8 Assessment

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR VISIT:
Mr. Gerald Holloway is a 58-year-old male with Stage IVA KRAS G12D-mutant, MSS sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with synchronous hepatic metastases, presenting for follow-up after completion of cycle 8 of modified FOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab. This visit incorporates the results of a formal multidisciplinary hepatobiliary tumor board review of his most recent cross-sectional imaging.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:

Mr. Holloway was diagnosed approximately seven months ago with Stage IVA sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma (pT3 pN2a M1a) with four synchronous hepatic metastases (segment VI, IVb, II, and VII), the largest measuring 3.1 cm at baseline. He underwent laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy with R0 resection approximately 5-6 weeks prior to initiating systemic therapy. Molecular profiling confirmed KRAS G12D mutation, MSS/pMMR status, BRAF wild-type, and NRAS wild-type, rendering him ineligible for anti-EGFR therapy.

He was started on first-line modified FOLFOX6 (oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2, leucovorin 400 mg/m2, 5-FU bolus 400 mg/m2, 5-FU infusion 2400 mg/m2 over 46 hours) plus bevacizumab 5 mg/kg every 14 days, with the explicit intent of cytoreduction toward potential hepatic resection. Treatment was initiated with a curative-intent conversion chemotherapy framework.

At the cycle 6 restaging CT (approximately three months into treatment), he demonstrated a meaningful partial response in all four hepatic lesions -- the segment VI index lesion shrank from 3.1 cm to 1.9 cm, segment IVb from 2.2 cm to 1.4 cm, segment II from 1.4 cm to 0.8 cm, and segment VII from 0.9 cm to 0.5 cm. Total target lesion burden declined approximately 39% from baseline. No new lesions were identified. His CEA trended down substantially from 187 ng/mL at diagnosis. This was a gratifying early response and the hepatobiliary surgery team was contacted for reassessment.

However, in the interval between cycles 6 and 8, Mr. Holloway developed worsening peripheral sensory neuropathy attributed to cumulative oxaliplatin exposure, progressing to grade 2 severity -- moderate persistent paresthesias in both hands and feet interfering with fine motor tasks such as buttoning shirts, as well as mild difficulty navigating uneven surfaces. As a result, the oxaliplatin dose was reduced by 25% to 65 mg/m2 beginning with cycle 7. His bevacizumab-associated hypertension also worsened during this period, requiring addition of amlodipine 5 mg daily to his existing lisinopril 20 mg, and is now better controlled.

He has now completed cycle 8. His most recent CEA is 52 ng/mL (down from 187 at baseline, though still elevated). He reports ongoing but tolerable fatigue. HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Mr. Holloway was initially diagnosed approximately 19 months ago with Stage IVA colorectal adenocarcinoma of the sigmoid colon (pT3 pN2a M1a) with synchronous hepatic metastases (four lesions, largest 3.1 cm in segment VI). His tumor harbors a KRAS p.G12D driver mutation (VAF 41% by NGS), APC and TP53 co-mutations, is microsatellite stable (MSS) by both IHC and NGS, TMB-low (6 mut/Mb), BRAF V600E wild-type, and HER2 non-amplified. These molecular characteristics preclude his eligibility for anti-EGFR therapy (cetuximab, panitumumab) and for single-agent immune checkpoint blockade.

Following laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy with R0 resection performed approximately 5 weeks after diagnosis (final pathology pT3 pN2a, 3/18 nodes, LVI and PNI present), he was started on first-line mFOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab (oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2, LV 400 mg/m2, 5-FU bolus 400 mg/m2 + 5-FU CI 2400 mg/m2 over 46 hours, bevacizumab 5 mg/kg, q2w) with intent to assess liver-directed resection after cytoreduction. He achieved a meaningful hepatic response by cycle 6 restaging (all four liver metastases decreased ~40%), and further deepened to ~55% reduction at cycle 10 restaging. However, cumulative oxaliplatin-related peripheral sensory neuropathy became dose-limiting at cycle 10 (grade 2-3 -- persistent bilateral hand and foot paresthesias with significant functional impairment), and oxaliplatin was discontinued. He transitioned to maintenance 5-FU/bevacizumab.

During maintenance, the hepatic lesions remained reasonably stable, but restaging CT at approximately 10 months from treatment start demonstrated radiographic progression -- modest growth of all four hepatic metastases and appearance of two new right-sided pulmonary nodules (RLL 0.8 cm and RUL 0.5 cm) with a concurrent rise in CEA from 22 to 41 ng/mL. At that time, hepatic resection was deferred given the emergence of bilateral pulmonary metastatic disease, and he was transitioned to second-line therapy.

He was started on FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab (irinotecan 180 mg/m2, LV 400 mg/m2, 5-FU bolus/CI per FOLFOX schedule, ramucirumab 8 mg/kg, q2w) approximately 10.5 months from initial diagnosis. His course on this regimen has been complicated by grade 2 diarrhea (managed with loperamide as needed), grade 2 alopecia, and a significant episode of grade 3 hypertension at cycles 8-9, requiring ramucirumab to be held for one cycle and intensification of his antihypertensive regimen (amlodipine uptitrated to 10 mg daily, hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg added). His UGT1A1 genotype was found to be *1/*28 (heterozygous), and he has been monitored closely for delayed-onset diarrhea throughout this regimen.

Initially, FOLFIRI/ramucirumab produced a partial response by cycle 6 restaging (hepatic lesions down ~17-23%, RLL nodule slightly decreased, CEA falling from 41 to 26 ng/mL). Restaging at cycle 12 (approximately 6 months on second-line therapy, 16 months from diagnosis) confirmed stable disease with continued disease control and CEA at 22 ng/mL. More compellingly, his CEA has risen sharply from 22 to 58 ng/mL since the last stable scan, and CA 19-9 has climbed from 19 to 47 U/mL. Taken together, this CT and the tumor marker trajectory confirm progressive disease on FOLFIRI/ramucirumab after approximately 9 months on this regimen.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Mr. Holloway is a 59-year-old male with progressive metastatic sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma, KRAS G12D-mutant, MSS, BRAF wild-type, HER2 non-amplified, with hepatic and bilateral pulmonary metastases. He has now exhausted two lines of standard combination chemotherapy:
-- First line: mFOLFOX6 + bevacizumab (cycles 1-10 with oxaliplatin, then maintenance 5-FU/bevacizumab) -- achieved partial response, limited by cumulative neuropathy; progressed at approximately 10 months.
-- Second line: FOLFIRI + ramucirumab (12 cycles, approximately 9 months) -- achieved partial response initially, now progressing clinically and radiographically.

He retains an ECOG PS of 1 to 2 (I would characterize today as a 2, given the increased fatigue and some functional decline, though he remains ambulatory and partially self-caring), adequate end-organ function, and he and his family are motivated and engaged in pursuing continued active treatment.

Given his molecular profile and treatment history, the following third-line options were discussed at length with the patient and his wife today:

1. TAS-102 (trifluridine/tipiracil, Lonsurf): An oral cytotoxic agent approved in the third-line and beyond for metastatic CRC. Data from the RECOURSE trial demonstrated modest but statistically significant improvement in overall survival (~7.1 months vs. ~5.3 months with placebo) in a heavily pre-treated population. Toxicity profile includes myelosuppression (neutropenia is the primary concern), fatigue, and nausea -- generally manageable. Does not require KRAS wild-type status. Given Mr. Holloway's adequate bone marrow reserve and organ function, this would be feasible.

2. Regorafenib (Stivarga): An oral multikinase inhibitor with activity in refractory mCRC. Also third-line and beyond indication. The ReDOS dosing strategy (dose escalation starting at 80 mg daily) is associated with improved tolerability compared to the standard 160 mg starting dose. Primary toxicities include hand-foot skin reaction, fatigue, hypertension, and hepatotoxicity. Given Mr. Holloway's existing hypertension and borderline liver enzyme elevation, this would require close monitoring and may be less ideal from a tolerability standpoint.

3. Clinical trial -- KRAS G12D-directed investigational therapy: The patient's tumor harbors a confirmed KRAS p.G12D mutation, which has been an area of intense drug development. Several early-phase trials are actively enrolling patients with KRAS G12D-mutant solid tumors. This represents a biologically rational approach and potentially the most compelling option for a molecularly selected patient like Mr. Holloway. I have identified a Phase I/II study at our regional cancer center evaluating a selective KRAS G12D inhibitor in patients with solid tumors including colorectal cancer. I discussed this at length, including the investigational nature of the therapy, the need to re-biopsy for correlative studies as per the trial protocol, and the logistical implications of treatment at an outside center.

After discussion, Mr. Holloway expressed strong interest in the clinical trial option. He and his wife understand the investigational nature and are willing to travel to the regional center. He is also amenable to TAS-102 as a bridge or fallback if trial screening is unsuccessful.

-- FOLFIRI/ramucirumab to be discontinued effective today given confirmed progressive disease.
-- Referral placed urgently to the clinical trial unit at the regional cancer center for screening and eligibility assessment for the KRAS G12D inhibitor trial. I have contacted the PI directly and will forward all relevant records, molecular reports, and imaging.
-- Re-biopsy of an accessible lesion (the right lower lobe pulmonary nodule is the most accessible site) will be required per the trial protocol for fresh correlative tissue. A CT-guided biopsy will be arranged through the trial site.
-- If clinical trial screening is not successful or enrollment is delayed beyond 4-6 weeks, we will initiate TAS-102 as third-line therapy in the interim.
-- Goals of care discussion conducted today. Patient and wife have been informed regarding the progressive nature of his disease after two lines of chemotherapy. He understands that his cancer is not curable with available standard therapies, but he wishes to continue active treatment with the goal of disease control and quality of life preservation. -- Blood pressure: Today's reading is concerning at 152/94 mmHg. Given that ramucirumab is being discontinued, we will observe over the coming weeks for improvement. If hypertension persists at this level off anti-angiogenic therapy, I will work with his primary care physician to reassess his antihypertensive regimen. Continue lisinopril 20 mg, amlodipine 10 mg, hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg for now.
-- Residual neuropathy: Continue monitoring. No specific interventions needed at this time; grade 1 residual sensory neuropathy from prior oxaliplatin is expected to persist but is tolerable.
-- Mild anemia (Hgb 10.6 g/dL): Does not require transfusion at this time. Consistent with anemia of chronic disease in the setting of ongoing malignancy. Will recheck CBC at next visit.
-- Mild transaminase elevation (ALT 61, AST 55, ALP 112): Likely multifactorial -- chronic disease, residual treatment effect, and possibly early metastatic hepatic parenchymal involvement. Will monitor closely; if rising ALP or bilirubin emerges, repeat imaging of the liver and further evaluation warranted.

-- Patient tolerated today's lengthy and difficult conversation well. His wife is engaged and supportive. He was provided written educational materials on clinical trial participation. While on maintenance, disease remained relatively stable through approximately 7 months but then progressed radiographically at approximately the 10-month mark, with modest hepatic lesion growth and the appearance of two new right-sided pulmonary nodules (right lower lobe 0.8 cm, right upper lobe 0.5 cm). CEA rose concurrently from 22 to 41 ng/mL. At that point, the molecular profile was reviewed: KRAS G12D mutation confirmed by prior NGS, MSS status, BRAF V600E wild-type, NRAS wild-type -- making him ineligible for anti-EGFR therapy, BRAF-targeted combinations, or immune checkpoint inhibitor monotherapy. He was transitioned to second-line FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab.

He initially responded well to FOLFIRI/ramucirumab, with partial response on cycle 6 restaging (hepatic and pulmonary lesions decreased, CEA dropped from 41 to 26 ng/mL). Disease remained in a stable/partial response state through the cycle 12 restaging approximately 6 months into second-line therapy (CEA 22 ng/mL, CA 19-9 19 U/mL). However, his course was complicated by grade 3 hypertension attributed primarily to ramucirumab at approximately cycle 8-9, requiring ramucirumab to be held for one cycle and the addition of a third antihypertensive agent (hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg); blood pressure subsequently improved and ramucirumab was resumed at full dose.

At the cycle 16 office visit approximately 3 months ago, his disease remained controlled per CT (stable disease), though he was noted to have mild anemia (hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL), cumulative fatigue, and mildly elevated transaminases (ALT 52, AST 48).

Today's visit is prompted by his cycle 18 restaging CT, completed within the last week. Imaging demonstrates clear progressive disease after approximately 9 months total on FOLFIRI/ramucirumab: hepatic metastases have grown modestly across all four segments, the right lower lobe pulmonary nodule has grown from 0.7 to 0.9 cm, and tumor markers have risen substantially -- CEA now 58 ng/mL (up from 22) and CA 19-9 47 U/mL (up from 19). No new sites of distant metastatic disease were identified (no peritoneal disease, no bone, no brain).

Mr. Holloway came in today with his wife. He reports increased fatigue over the past 4-6 weeks, which he had been attributing to the chemotherapy. He also notes mild increase in abdominal bloating and a 4-pound unintentional weight loss over the past month. He denies new pain, cough, dyspnea, hemoptysis, melena, or hematochezia. Neuropathy from prior oxaliplatin has continued to slowly improve and is now grade 1 (mild, persistent distal foot numbness, not functionally limiting). He remains ambulatory and independent in all activities of daily living but acknowledges he is more tired than he was 6 months ago.

PLAN:

Disease Management -- Third-Line Systemic Therapy:
Mr. Holloway and his wife were both present for this discussion, which was held at length. I explained clearly that the CT results show that the current treatment regimen (FOLFIRI/ramucirumab) is no longer holding the cancer in check -- the liver spots and lung spots have grown and the tumor markers have risen significantly. He understands this means we need to change treatment.

We reviewed the following options in the context of his molecular profile and treatment history:

(a) TAS-102 (trifluridine/tipiracil, brand name Lonsurf): An oral chemotherapy approved for refractory metastatic colorectal cancer. Given twice daily on days 1-5 and 8-12 of each 28-day cycle. Main side effects include myelosuppression (particularly neutropenia), fatigue, nausea. The addition of bevacizumab to TAS-102 (as in the SUNLIGHT trial) has shown improved survival outcomes compared to TAS-102 alone in the refractory setting. Given he has previously been exposed to bevacizumab (not to ramucirumab specifically, though both target VEGF pathway), bevacizumab re-challenge in combination with TAS-102 is a reasonable consideration, pending blood pressure stability.

(b) Regorafenib (Stivarga): An oral multikinase inhibitor approved in the refractory colorectal setting. Given daily on days 1-21 of each 28-day cycle, typically with dose escalation per ReDOS strategy starting at 80 mg/day. Side effects include hand-foot skin reaction, fatigue, hypertension, hepatotoxicity, and diarrhea. Given his existing hypertension management needs and mildly elevated transaminases, regorafenib carries somewhat more concern, but remains a legitimate option.

(c) Clinical trial -- KRAS G12D-directed investigational therapy: The patient's KRAS G12D mutation has historically been considered undruggable, but several KRAS G12D-selective small molecule inhibitors are now in early clinical development (Phase I/II). I discussed with Mr. Holloway the possibility of enrolling in a clinical trial targeting KRAS G12D. There is a Phase I/II trial currently open at a regional cancer center approximately 45 minutes from his home, evaluating an investigational oral KRAS G12D inhibitor in patients with KRAS G12D-mutant solid tumors including colorectal cancer. Prior lines of therapy (2 lines completed) would make him potentially eligible, pending full screening. This represents an opportunity to access novel, molecularly targeted therapy tailored to his specific driver mutation, with the potential for meaningful disease control. The experimental nature and uncertainties of Phase I/II trial participation were explained in full.

After thorough discussion, Mr. Holloway expressed strong preference to pursue clinical trial enrollment as his next step -- he is motivated, has a KRAS G12D mutation confirmed on both primary and metastatic tissue, understands the investigational nature of the treatment, and is willing to travel. He would like TAS-102 (with or without bevacizumab) held in reserve as a subsequent option if trial therapy fails or he becomes ineligible.

Plan: Referral to the clinical trial unit at the regional cancer center will be placed today. I will contact the trial coordinator directly. The patient will undergo re-biopsy of an accessible metastatic lesion (right lower lobe pulmonary nodule, amenable to CT-guided biopsy) as required for trial enrollment re-biopsy/correlative studies -- referral to interventional radiology placed for CT-guided lung biopsy, to be scheduled at the trial site or here depending on trial protocol.

Goals of Care:
I had a frank, compassionate discussion with Mr. Holloway and his wife today regarding the overall disease trajectory. He has now progressed on two lines of standard systemic therapy. His disease remains limited (hepatic and pulmonary metastases only, no peritoneal or bone involvement) and his performance status, though slightly declined from peak, remains reasonable (ECOG 1-2). He confirms his primary goal remains disease control with preservation of quality of life. He has thought about the future and acknowledges that at some point, the balance of burden of treatment versus benefit may shift. He is not ready to discuss hospice at this time, and this is entirely appropriate given remaining treatment options. We discussed that if additional therapies are not providing benefit or his performance status declines further, we will revisit goals of care at that time. He was reassured that palliative and supportive care services remain available at any point.

Patient was given printed information on TAS-102/Lonsurf as a backup option and information about clinical trial participation. He and his wife were encouraged to call with any questions. His wife expressed gratitude for the thorough explanation.

Blood pressure 144/90 today -- slightly above target. Current regimen: lisinopril 20 mg daily, amlodipine 10 mg daily, hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg daily. Will continue current regimen. If anti-angiogenic agent continued in next line, very close BP monitoring warranted. If BP worsens off ramucirumab (which it may improve on cessation), medication adjustments can be made.

MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION

Sections from cassette A (cores 1 and 2) show fragments of lung parenchyma with near-complete replacement by a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma. The neoplastic cells are arranged in irregular glandular and cribriform structures, with focal solid nesting and central comedonecrosis ("dirty necrosis" pattern). Individual tumor cells are columnar to cuboidal with moderately enlarged, hyperchromatic nuclei, prominent nucleoli, and a moderate amount of amphophilic cytoplasm. Intraluminal necrotic debris is present within glandular lumina, a morphologic pattern highly characteristic of colorectal-type adenocarcinoma. Mitotic figures are readily identified (estimated 6-8 per 10 high-power fields). Background viable lung parenchyma is present at the core periphery, showing mild reactive type II pneumocyte hyperplasia and focal hemosiderin deposition consistent with prior procedural change; no primary lung carcinoma morphology (no lepidic, acinar-predominant primary pattern, no squamous differentiation, no neuroendocrine features) is identified.

There is no evidence of visceral pleural involvement in the sampled material. Lymphovascular invasion is identified focally within the biopsy fragments. Perineural invasion is not identified in the sampled material.

IMMUNOHISTOCHEMISTRY (ANCILLARY STUDIES)

A directed immunohistochemical panel was performed to characterize the tumor and confirm colorectal primary origin:

  CK20 (clone Ks20.8): POSITIVE -- diffuse strong cytoplasmic/membranous staining in tumor cells.
  CDX2 (clone EPR2764Y): POSITIVE -- strong diffuse nuclear staining in tumor cells.
  CK7 (clone OV-TL 12/30): FOCALLY POSITIVE -- weak patchy staining in a minority of tumor cells.
  TTF-1 (clone 8G7G3/1): NEGATIVE -- no nuclear staining in tumor cells (positive internal control in background pneumocytes).
  Napsin A: NEGATIVE -- no cytoplasmic staining in tumor cells.
  p40: NEGATIVE -- no nuclear staining in tumor cells.
  Synaptophysin: NEGATIVE.
  Chromogranin A: NEGATIVE.
  SATB2 (clone EP281): POSITIVE -- nuclear staining in tumor cells (supports lower gastrointestinal origin).

IMPRESSION (IHC): The immunophenotype (CK20+, CDX2+, SATB2+, CK7 focally+, TTF-1 negative, Napsin A negative, p40 negative) is consistent with metastatic adenocarcinoma of colorectal origin. A primary pulmonary adenocarcinoma is not supported by the morphologic and immunohistochemical findings.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Holloway is a 59-year-old former smoker (20 pack-year history, quit 12 years ago) with a known diagnosis of Stage IVA (pT3 pN2a M1a) moderately differentiated sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma, initially diagnosed approximately 21 months ago. His disease was characterized at diagnosis by KRAS G12D mutation (VAF 41%), MSS status by both IHC and NGS, BRAF wild-type, NRAS wild-type, and co-mutations in APC and TP53. HER2 was not amplified. TMB was low (6 mut/Mb). At diagnosis he had synchronous liver metastases (4 lesions, largest 3.1 cm in segment VI).

He underwent laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy with primary anastomosis approximately 5 weeks before starting systemic therapy, achieving an R0 resection (pT3 pN2a, 3/18 lymph nodes positive, lymphovascular and perineural invasion present). The liver metastases were left in situ at the time of surgery.

First-line systemic therapy: He was treated with modified FOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab, initiated approximately 16 months ago. He achieved a partial response to FOLFOX/bev, with his four hepatic lesions shrinking considerably (segment VI from 3.1 cm to as small as 1.5 cm at best response). However, he developed cumulative oxaliplatin-related peripheral sensory neuropathy that ultimately progressed to grade 2-3 functional impairment, prompting discontinuation of oxaliplatin after cycle 10. He was then transitioned to maintenance 5-FU/bevacizumab. Neuropathy began to gradually improve off oxaliplatin. Hypertension was also a notable toxicity, requiring uptitration of his antihypertensive regimen from lisinopril monotherapy to triple-agent therapy (lisinopril 20 mg, amlodipine 10 mg, hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg). Approximately 10 months from treatment initiation, restaging CT demonstrated progression -- slight growth in hepatic lesions and the emergence of 2 new right-sided pulmonary nodules, with a concurrent rise in CEA from 22 to 41 ng/mL. His nadir CEA on first-line therapy had been approximately 22 ng/mL.

Second-line systemic therapy: He was switched to FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab approximately 10.5 months from initial treatment start. He again achieved a partial response to this regimen, with both hepatic and pulmonary lesions responding. He tolerated the regimen with manageable toxicity -- grade 2 diarrhea controlled with loperamide, grade 2 alopecia (expected with irinotecan), and grade 3 hypertension during cycles 8-9 that required a brief hold on ramucirumab and further antihypertensive intensification (amlodipine increased from 5 mg to 10 mg, and hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg added). UGT1A1 genotyping showed *1/*28 heterozygosity (intermediate metabolizer). Disease remained in control for approximately 9 months on FOLFIRI/ramucirumab. Restaging CT at approximately 19 months from initial diagnosis demonstrated disease progression -- slow but definite growth in hepatic lesions (aggregate about 23% increase from nadir) and growth of the right lower lobe pulmonary nodule from 0.7 cm to 0.9 cm, with rising CEA (22 ng/mL nadir rising to 58 ng/mL). No new metastatic sites were identified.

At that point, given KRAS G12D mutation, the patient remained ineligible for anti-EGFR therapy (cetuximab, panitumumab). His MSS status precluded immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy. He was referred to the investigational drug service and underwent screening for the current Phase I/II trial of an oral, selective KRAS G12D inhibitor. Re-biopsy of the right lower lobe pulmonary nodule confirmed metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma (CK20+, CDX2+, TTF-1 negative) with preserved KRAS G12D mutation (VAF 38%) and no new acquired resistance mutations (NRAS, BRAF, KRAS amplification all negative), confirming ongoing KRAS G12D-driven disease. He enrolled approximately 1.5 months ago and is currently completing his third cycle of the investigational agent.

He reports that overall, he has tolerated the oral investigational agent acceptably. He describes a mild, diffuse skin rash that appeared during week 2 of treatment, which was managed with topical clindamycin gel applied twice daily and has improved. He notes mild nausea, primarily in the first 1-2 hours after taking the morning dose, which he manages by taking the medication with a small amount of food. He has not required any dose interruptions. He denies vomiting, significant diarrhea, oral mucositis, or dyspnea. He notes mild fatigue, which he rates as 3/10 and considers manageable -- he has been able to perform light household activities and short walks. His residual neuropathy, primarily numbness in the soles of his feet, has continued to slowly improve off oxaliplatin and is now grade 1 and non-limiting. He notes his appetite is fair; he has lost approximately 2-3 pounds since starting this trial, which he attributes to mild nausea rather than anorexia. He has been monitoring his blood pressure at home and reports readings in the range of 130-140/84-90 mmHg on his current triple antihypertensive regimen.

He is hopeful. He mentions that his CEA has been heading in a better direction and is curious about the upcoming CT scan results.

SNVs AND SMALL INDELS

1. KRAS p.G12D (c.35G>A)
   Gene function: Proto-oncogene; RAS GTPase; central node of MAPK/ERK and PI3K signaling
   Variant type: Missense substitution
   Exon: 2
   Variant allele frequency (VAF): 41%
   Coverage at locus: 812x
   Classification: Pathogenic / Oncogenic Driver (Tier IA)
   Notes: KRAS codon 12 mutations are among the most common oncogenic alterations in colorectal adenocarcinoma, occurring in approximately 40% of cases. The G12D substitution (Gly→Asp) impairs intrinsic GTPase activity of the KRAS protein, resulting in constitutive activation of RAS-MAPK and PI3K-AKT signaling cascades. This specific mutation is present in approximately 12-13% of all colorectal cancers and approximately 30% of KRAS-mutant CRCs. KRAS G12D is a negative predictive biomarker for response to anti-EGFR monoclonal antibodies (cetuximab and panitumumab); such therapy is contraindicated. KRAS G12D-selective small molecule inhibitors (e.g., MRTX1133 and related compounds) are currently under evaluation in Phase I/II clinical trials, and this mutation may confer eligibility for such trials.

2. APC p.R1450* (c.4348C>T)
   Gene function: Tumor suppressor; Wnt/beta-catenin pathway regulator
   Variant type: Nonsense (stop-gain)
   Exon: 16 (mutation cluster region)
   VAF: 44%
   Coverage at locus: 788x
   Classification: Pathogenic / Loss-of-function (Tier IB)
   Notes: APC truncating mutations are the earliest and most common somatic events in sporadic colorectal tumorigenesis, identified in over 80% of colorectal adenocarcinomas. The p.R1450* variant results in premature truncation of the APC protein, abolishing its ability to form the beta-catenin destruction complex and resulting in constitutive nuclear beta-catenin signaling. The near-equal VAF to that of KRAS suggests this represents a co-occurring founder/clonal event, consistent with the classical adenoma-to-carcinoma sequence in sporadic CRC. No germline APC variant is reported; absence of polyposis phenotype (no synchronous adenomatous polyps on colonoscopy) is consistent with somatic, sporadic acquisition.

3. TP53 p.R175H (c.524G>A)
   Gene function: Tumor suppressor; transcription factor; genomic guardian
   Variant type: Missense substitution (gain-of-function hotspot)
   Exon: 5
   VAF: 39%
   Coverage at locus: 804x
   Classification: Pathogenic / Gain-of-function oncogenic (Tier IB)
   Notes: TP53 p.R175H is one of the most well-characterized gain-of-function (GOF) hotspot mutations in human cancer. Unlike simple loss-of-function TP53 mutations, the R175H variant not only abrogates wild-type p53 tumor suppressor function but also endows the mutant protein with neomorphic oncogenic activities, including promotion of invasion, resistance to apoptosis, and dysregulation of gene expression programs. This mutation is detected in approximately 10-15% of colorectal cancers. The VAF of 39% is consistent with clonal acquisition. TP53 alterations in CRC generally indicate poor prognosis and are associated with chromosomal instability (CIN) phenotype, consistent with the copy number alterations detected in this specimen.

- The APC p.R1450* variant detected at VAF 44% is interpreted as a somatic founder mutation based on clinical context (no FAP phenotype, no synchronous adenomatous polyposis on colonoscopy, no family history of CRC or polyposis)
- BRCA1/2 wild-type; no actionable homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) alterations identified
- Genetic counseling referral and germline testing may be considered per institutional protocol if clinically indicated, though the overall molecular profile is consistent with sporadic CRC

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
MOLECULAR SUBTYPE CLASSIFICATION
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Consensus Molecular Subtype (CMS): Most consistent with CMS2 (Canonical/Epithelial)
  - Features supporting CMS2: MSS, chromosomal instability (CIN), APC driver mutation, TP53 mutation, 20q gain, MYC amplification, absence of BRAF V600E, high WNT/MYC transcriptional activation
  - CMS2 accounts for approximately 37% of CRCs and is characterized by epithelial differentiation, CIN, and activation of WNT and MYC signaling
  - CMS1 (MSI/immune) excluded: MSS, low TMB
  - CMS3 (metabolic) less likely: KRAS G12D present, but no PIK3CA or metabolic gene enrichment
  - CMS4 (mesenchymal) less likely: no TGF-beta pathway activation markers, no SMAD4 loss

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
RAS PATHWAY COMPREHENSIVE ANALYSIS
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
KRAS exons 2-4 (codons 12, 13, 59, 61, 117, 146): KRAS p.G12D DETECTED (VAF 41%); all other codons wild-type
NRAS exons 2-4 (codons 12, 13, 59, 61, 117, 146): Wild-type
HRAS exons 2-4: Wild-type
BRAF exon 11, 15 (V600E and non-V600 hotspots including G469A, L597, K601, D594): Wild-type
NF1: Wild-type

RAS/RAF pathway conclusion: RAS pathway activation is mediated exclusively through KRAS p.G12D. BRAF wild-type status (including V600E negativity) excludes eligibility for BRAF+MEK inhibitor combinations (e.g., encorafenib/binimetinib as used in BRAF V600E-mutant CRC). The extended RAS panel (all codons per ESMO/ASCO guidelines) confirms full KRAS and NRAS mutational status. The patient is NOT eligible for anti-EGFR therapy (cetuximab/panitumumab) based on KRAS exon 2 mutation.

  - Anti-EGFR therapy (cetuximab/panitumumab): CONTRAINDICATED -- KRAS exon 2 mutation (p.G12D)

MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION:

Sections of the primary tumor (specimen A) show an invasive adenocarcinoma with moderately differentiated (G2) glandular architecture. The tumor is composed of irregular, angulated neoplastic glands with stratified nuclei exhibiting moderate nuclear pleomorphism, prominent nucleoli, and numerous mitotic figures. Focal areas of intraluminal "dirty necrosis" (luminal necrosis with nuclear debris) are noted, a characteristic feature of conventional colorectal adenocarcinoma. There is no mucinous component (less than 10% extracellular mucin). No signet ring cells are identified.

At the invasive front, the tumor extends through the full thickness of the muscularis propria into the pericolorectal adipose tissue as irregular jagged tongues of neoplastic glands. The visceral peritoneum/serosal surface is examined on multiple levels and does NOT show tumor cells at the peritoneal surface; the closest approach of tumor to the serosal surface is estimated at greater than 1 mm on histologic sections. The circumferential (radial) inked margin is clear of tumor by 4 mm at the point of deepest invasion.

Tumor budding at the invasive front is prominent and scored as Bd3 (high-grade budding, greater than 10 tumor buds per standardized high-power field), consistent with increased invasive and metastatic potential.

Extensive lymphovascular invasion is identified: multiple tumor cell clusters are present within thin-walled endothelium-lined spaces in the pericolorectal adipose tissue. Immunohistochemistry with D2-40 (podoplanin) highlights intralymphatic tumor emboli in at least 5 separate foci.

Perineural invasion is present: tumor cells are identified surrounding and tracking along large-caliber nerve fibers within pericolorectal fat.

One pericolorectal tumor deposit is identified as a discrete irregular nodule of adenocarcinoma in the pericolorectal adipose tissue measuring 0.6 cm in greatest dimension, without residual lymph node architecture or vascular wall, consistent with tumor deposit (TD category per AJCC 8th edition).

Three of the 18 regional lymph nodes examined (including 17 from specimen A and 1 apical node from specimen B) contain metastatic adenocarcinoma. The three positive nodes measure 1.1 cm, 0.8 cm, and 0.5 cm in greatest dimension; all show partial to near-complete replacement by metastatic carcinoma. Extranodal extension is NOT identified in any of the involved nodes. The remaining 15 lymph nodes are negative for metastatic carcinoma, showing reactive changes only.

The proximal and distal surgical margins (en face sections, A6 and A7) and both anastomotic donut rings (C1 and C2) are negative for malignancy, confirmed on permanent sections.

Background sigmoid and uninvolved colonic mucosa shows mild reactive cryptitis without dysplasia. No adenomatous polyps, serrated polyps, or additional primary neoplasms are identified. Mild diverticular change with mucosal herniation is noted in the adjacent sigmoid. No evidence of inflammatory bowel disease.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
IMPRESSION / SUMMARY:

Sigmoid colon: Moderately differentiated (G2) invasive adenocarcinoma, conventional type, pT3 pN2a pM1a (Stage IVA, AJCC 8th edition). R0 resection with all margins negative. Extensive lymphovascular invasion and perineural invasion present. High-grade tumor budding (Bd3). One tumor deposit. Intact mismatch repair protein expression (MSS). KRAS G12D mutation confirmed on this resection specimen. Findings are consistent with the known metastatic colorectal carcinoma in this clinical context.

Mr. Holloway initially presented to his primary care physician approximately 6-7 weeks ago with several months of progressive lower abdominal cramping, a change in bowel habits (alternating loose stools and constipation), and intermittent hematochezia. He denied significant unintentional weight loss at initial presentation, though he endorses approximately 8 lbs of weight loss over the past 3 months in retrospect. Colonoscopy was performed and revealed a partially obstructing mass in the sigmoid colon. Colonoscopic biopsies confirmed moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma. Staging CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast demonstrated the primary sigmoid mass measuring 4.8 x 3.9 cm, along with 4 hepatic metastatic lesions -- the largest measuring 3.1 cm in hepatic segment VI, with additional lesions in segments IVb (2.2 cm), II (1.4 cm), and VII (0.9 cm). Mr. Holloway is a 58-year-old male civil engineer, ECOG PS 1, with Stage IVA moderately differentiated sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with synchronous hepatic metastases (4 lesions), now approximately 5 weeks post-laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy with R0 resection (pT3 pN2a). Molecular profile: KRAS p.G12D mutant, NRAS/BRAF wild-type, MSS/pMMR, HER2 non-amplified, TMB-low. He is here today to discuss systemic therapy for his metastatic disease.

1. METASTATIC COLORECTAL CANCER -- SYSTEMIC THERAPY DISCUSSION

Given his molecular profile, several important biomarker-driven exclusions apply:
- KRAS G12D mutation renders him ineligible for anti-EGFR therapy (Erbitux, Vectibix/pani). This applies regardless of tumor sidedness.
- MSS/pMMR status excludes him from benefit with immune checkpoint inhibitors (pembro, Opdivo) per current evidence base for CRC.
- BRAF wild-type means the BRAF-MEK targeted doublet (Braftovi/Mektovi + cetux) is not applicable.
- KRAS G12D-specific inhibitors remain investigational and are not yet commercially available; clinical trial enrollment could be revisited at later disease stages if available.
- No actionable fusions or other targets were identified on comprehensive NGS.

For first-line treatment of metastatic colorectal cancer in a patient with good PS and resectable or potentially convertible hepatic disease, standard-of-care systemic therapy consists of a doublet chemotherapy backbone with an anti-VEGF/VEGFR agent. I discussed the following regimens:

- FOLFOX (oxali + leucovorin + 5-FU) plus bev (Avastin)
- FOLFIRI (iri + leucovorin + 5-FU) plus Avastin
- CAPOX (cape + oxali) plus bev

After thorough discussion with Mr. Holloway and his wife, I am recommending modified FOLFOX6 plus Avastin as the first-line regimen. Rationale:
- FOLFOX backbone has demonstrated favorable efficacy and tolerability in the first-line metastatic CRC setting; oxali-based regimens are standard in conversion therapy intent given data supporting higher hepatic resection rates
- bev (anti-VEGF) has been well-validated in combination with FOLFOX for overall survival benefit in metastatic CRC regardless of RAS mutation status
- CAPOX is a reasonable alternative but IV infusional 5-FU (FOLFOX) gives us the flexibility to dose-modify each component individually; pump-delivered continuous infusion 5-FU is familiar to this patient and preferred by our nursing team
- Mr. Holloway has adequate cardiac, renal, and hepatic function to tolerate Avastin; blood pressure is borderline elevated and will be closely monitored and managed

Regimen (planned):
- Eloxatin 85 mg/m2 IV day 1
- Leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV day 1
- 5FU 400 mg/m2 IV bolus day 1
- 5-FU 2400 mg/m2 IV continuous infusion over 46 hours (via portable pump)
- bev 5 mg/kg IV day 1
- Cycle length: 14 days (every 2 weeks)
- Treatment intent: Disease control with goal of achieving sufficient hepatic response to render the liver metastases resectable; plan for reassessment with hepatobiliary surgery after 6-8 cycles

I spent considerable time reviewing expected toxicities with Mr. Holloway and his wife:
- Peripheral sensory neuropathy (Eloxatin-related, cumulative): cold-induced acral paresthesias early on; progressive sensory changes with prolonged use -- I counseled him to avoid cold exposure, particularly cold liquids and cold environmental exposure in the first 24-48 hours after each cycle. Neuropathy is the primary dose-limiting toxicity of Eloxatin and may require dose reduction or discontinuation with cumulative treatment.
- Nausea and vomiting: typically well-managed with modern antiemetics (ondansetron, prochlorperazine, dexamethasone)
- Myelosuppression: CBC will be monitored prior to each cycle; he will be instructed regarding neutropenic precautions and fever reporting
- Fatigue: common; likely to worsen with cumulative cycles
- Mucositis/hand-foot syndrome: less prominent with IV 5-FU than oral Xeloda
- Avastin-specific risks:
  * Hypertension: Mr. Holloway already has borderline elevated blood pressure on lisinopril 10 mg; Avastin frequently worsens hypertension and we will escalate antihypertensive therapy as needed. Close BP monitoring at each visit.
  * Wound healing impairment: bev must be held until adequate post-operative wound healing. Given he is approximately 5 weeks post-surgery and wounds appear well-healed, I am comfortable initiating bev at cycle 1. Standard of practice requires at least 4-6 weeks post-operatively; we meet this threshold.
  * Thromboembolism: both arterial (stroke, MI) and venous (DVT, PE) risks are modestly elevated with bev. Patient will be educated on signs/symptoms. Low-dose aspirin was discussed; I will defer to surgical team regarding resumption timing.
  * Proteinuria: urine dipstick monitoring each cycle; if significant proteinuria develops, dose hold per protocol.
  * GI perforation/fistula: rare but serious; patient instructed to report any acute abdominal pain, particularly given anastomosis site. Bowel continuity appears intact at this time and there were no intraoperative complications.
  * Bleeding: rare epistaxis is most common; serious bleeding uncommon in CRC population without major coagulopathy
- I also discussed hair thinning (mild with FOLFOX vs. prominent with iri) and the cumulative fatigue of bi-weekly therapy

Patient verbalized understanding and agreed to proceed. Informed consent obtained and documented.

2. HEPATOBILIARY MULTIDISCIPLINARY REVIEW

I have formally reviewed the pre-operative and current hepatic imaging with Dr. [hepatobiliary surgery attending] in the context of the multidisciplinary tumor board. The four hepatic lesions are potentially amenable to curative-intent resection if adequate oncologic response is achieved and future liver remnant (FLR) is preserved. Currently, the lesion distribution (bilobar, 4 lesions) and the need to preserve sufficient hepatic parenchyma makes upfront resection unfavorable without cytoreduction. After 6-8 cycles of systemic therapy with an adequate response, repeat imaging will be obtained and hepatobiliary surgery will re-evaluate. Portal vein embolization and staged hepatectomy will be discussed at that time as needed. This goal of potential surgical conversion is a key driver of the chosen regimen and treatment intensity.

3. TUMOR MARKERS

Baseline CEA 187 ng/mL and CA 19-9 94 U/mL are established. Both will be monitored at each cycle and are expected to trend down with effective therapy. Rising markers in the context of clinical stability should prompt consideration of earlier restaging imaging.

4. SUPPORTIVE CARE AND ANCILLARY REFERRALS

- Antiemetics: Ondansetron 8 mg IV pre-chemotherapy, dexamethasone 12 mg IV day 1. Take-home ondansetron 8 mg orally every 8 hours days 1-3, prochlorperazine 10 mg orally as needed for breakthrough nausea.
- Bowel: Loperamide on hand for diarrhea management. Continue fiber-rich diet as tolerated.
- Nutritional support: Mild albumin decline (3.7 g/dL) noted; referred to oncology dietitian for optimization of protein and caloric intake.
- Social work: Referral placed for social work support given new Stage IV diagnosis, occupational concerns, and financial/insurance navigation for bev (Avastin is typically covered under Part B/commercial plans; patient navigator will assist).
- Palliative care: Discussed the role of palliative care as a collaborative partner in symptom management -- not synonymous with hospice. Early integration palliative care referral placed per our institutional protocol for patients initiating systemic therapy for metastatic disease.
- Hypertension management: Will monitor BP at every cycle visit. I am not escalating antihypertensive therapy today given BP of 138/86 mmHg is borderline; however, patient instructed to monitor BP at home twice daily for the first 4 weeks on bev. Will work with primary care or escalate within oncology service as needed.
- Aspirin: Deferred; will discuss with surgery regarding safe timing to resume.
- Low molecular weight heparin (LMWH) VTE prophylaxis: Given Stage IV cancer, will discuss Khorana score and individualized VTE risk at first treatment visit; institutional guideline supports considering prophylactic anticoagulation in high-risk patients -- will revisit after surgery team sign-off.

5. FERTILITY / GONADAL TOXICITY

Mr. Holloway is 58 years old and does not express concern regarding fertility preservation. This was briefly discussed and he declined further evaluation.

6. TREATMENT SCHEDULE

Cycle 1 will be scheduled approximately 1 week from today to allow time for insurance authorization, placement of a port (or use of existing IV access -- patient currently has PICC line placed by surgery team, which will be used for cycle 1; port placement will be scheduled thereafter), and pre-treatment labs. Baseline CBC, CMP, LFTs, urine dipstick, CEA, and CA 19-9 will be obtained on cycle 1 day 1 prior to administration.

Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis will be performed after approximately cycle 6 (3 months of therapy), with interim evaluation by hepatobiliary surgery at that time.

7. GOALS OF CARE

I had a direct and compassionate conversation with Mr. Holloway and his wife regarding the nature of Stage IVA metastatic colorectal cancer. I was honest that the goal of systemic therapy at this stage is primarily to control disease, reduce tumor burden, and potentially render the liver metastases resectable -- which, if achieved, could substantially improve long-term outcomes. I also discussed that cure with systemic therapy alone is unlikely, though durable disease control is achievable in a meaningful subset of patients. Mr. Holloway expressed a clear preference for aggressive treatment and a desire to explore all options to achieve resection. He is emotionally engaged, asking appropriate questions, and has a supportive family present. He understands this is a marathon, not a sprint, and is committed to participating in a longitudinal treatment program.

Follow-up: Cycle 1 of modified FOLFOX6 + Avastin scheduled as above. Will be seen in clinic on day 1 of each cycle. 24-hour oncology on-call number provided for urgent concerns including fever, uncontrolled nausea/vomiting, severe abdominal pain, or signs of infection.
